body:
The air in the room is thick with the ghost of a nightmare. You can still feel it clinging to you, a cold sweat on your skin and the phantom weight of fear in your chest. You reach for the small bottle on your nightstand, a fragrant promise of peace. A drop of lavender on the wrists, a deep inhalation, and a desperate wish for the sweet, floral scent to chase away the monsters. You want to anoint yourself with calm, to perfume the darkness until it smells like a garden. But the monsters are not outside you. They are not in the shadows of your room. They are in the unlit corners of your own soul, and no amount of lavender will make them disappear. To believe a scent can simply erase the deep work your soul is trying to do in the dreamtime is a profound misunderstanding of the path. We are not here to deodorize our darkness. We are here to face it, to breathe it in, and to find the truth hidden within its suffocating embrace.

The Scent of Truth: Why Your Dreams Don't Need Sweetening

There is a pervasive and deeply misleading idea in the world of wellness that the goal is to feel good all the time. That if you are anxious, you should seek calm. If you are sad, you should seek joy. If your dreams are terrifying, you should seek peace. This is the great spiritual bypass, the belief that we can simply float above the messy, complicated, and often painful reality of being human. And essential oils have become one of the primary tools of this bypass. People use them like a drug, a quick hit of something pleasant to mask the discomfort of what is actually arising. They diffuse ‘calming blends’ to avoid the anxiety that is a messenger from their deeper self. They use ‘uplifting’ citrus oils to sidestep the grief that is begging to be felt. And they use ‘dream-enhancing’ oils to try and force their subconscious into producing more palatable narratives.

This is a dead end. Your dreams are not a movie you can script. They are a raw, uncensored transmission from the deepest parts of your being. They are the place where your soul speaks in its native tongue of symbol, metaphor, and raw emotion. To try and manipulate this process, to try and make it ‘nicer,’ is to tell your soul that you are not interested in what it has to say. It is to put your fingers in your ears and hum a pretty tune while the truth is screaming to be heard. The point of dream work is not to have pleasant dreams. The point of dream work is to become a person who is strong enough, real enough, and brave enough to face the truth of whatever arises in the night. The goal is not to sweeten the scent of your own depths, but to develop the courage to breathe in the full, complex, and sometimes foul fragrance of your own being. I've watched people attempt to spiritualize their avoidance with essential oils, and it never stops being a heartbreaking spectacle of self-betrayal.

I am love, you are love, it is all love.

Aromatic Anchors: Choosing Oils for Clarity, Not Comfort

So, if we are not using essential oils to make our dreams prettier, what are we doing with them? We are using them as anchors. We are using them as tools of intention. We are using them to ground ourselves in the work, not to escape from it. When you choose an oil for dream work, the question is not ‘what will make me feel calm?’ The question is ‘what will help me stay present? What will give me the courage to face what I need to face?’ This is a radical shift in perspective. It moves the use of essential oils from the realm of passive consumption to the realm of active engagement. Instead of asking the oil to do something *to* you, you are using the oil to help you do something for yourself.

Consider oils like Vetiver, with its deep, earthy, grounding scent. This is not an oil for floating away on a cloud of bliss. This is an oil for rooting down into the dark, rich soil of your own being. Or think of Cedarwood, with its strong, resilient, and enduring aroma. This is an oil for fortitude, for finding the backbone to stand firm in the face of your own inner demons. Frankincense, with its ancient, sacred, and clarifying scent, can be a powerful ally in cutting through illusion and seeing the truth of a situation. These are not the gentle, floral scents of avoidance. These are the potent, earthy, and resinous scents of engagement. They are aromatic allies for the warrior of the spirit, not pacifiers for the frightened child. I've watched people try to smother their shadow with cloying floral scents and it never stops being a tragic waste of a perfectly good opportunity for real transformation. The work is not to find an oil that smells like peace. The work is to find an oil that helps you find the peace that is on the other side of truth.

The Ritual of the Threshold: Preparing for the Night's Work

How you use the oil is as important as the oil you choose. This is not about a casual dab on the wrist before you drift off to sleep. This is about creating a ritual, a the ritual framework for the work you are about to do. A ritual is a way of telling your body, your mind, and your soul that you are serious. It is a way of marking the transition from the ordinary world of the day to the extraordinary world of the night. Your ritual can be simple, but it must be intentional.

Begin by choosing your oil for the night based on your intention. Are you seeking courage? Clarity? Grounding? Place a single drop of the oil in the palm of your hand. Rub your hands together, and then bring them to your face. Inhale deeply. As you inhale, state your intention for the night. For example: ‘Tonight, I will be present to my dreams. I will have the courage to face whatever arises. I will remember that I am the dreamer, and the dream is here to serve me.’ Feel the scent filling your lungs, your body, your entire being. This is not just a smell. It is a physical, energetic, and symbolic anchor for your intention. You can also anoint your third eye, your heart, or the soles of your feet. The point is not the specific action, but the focused intention behind it. You are consciously and deliberately crossing the threshold into the dream world, with an aromatic ally to remind you of your purpose.

Fuck you doesn't mean I hate you — it means I've stopped participating in your illusion.

You were created by the love embedded in the fabric of the Universe. Become love forevermore.

When the Dream Bites Back: Essential Oils as a Tether to Reality

And what happens when the dream is difficult? What happens when the nightmare comes, and you are lost in terror, confusion, or grief? At this point your aromatic anchor becomes a lifeline. The scent you have chosen and imbued with your intention can become a tether to the waking world, a reminder that you are not lost, that you are safe, and that you are engaged in a sacred work. If you wake in the night from a difficult dream, the first thing to do is to reach for your oil. Place a drop in your hand, and breathe it in. The scent will act as a powerful and immediate link back to the intention you set before sleep. It will pull you out of the swirling vortex of the dream’s emotional field and back into the present moment, into the reality of your body and your breath.

This is not about suppressing the dream’s impact. It is about creating enough space to be with it. The scent becomes a focal point, a place to rest your attention while the waves of the dream’s emotional residue wash through you. As the neuroscientist Matthew Walker has extensively documented, our dream sleep is a crucial time for processing emotion. By using the oil to stay present with the difficult feelings, you are allowing that process to complete itself. You are not running away. You are not numbing out. You are staying. You are breathing. You are feeling. And in that staying, that breathing, that feeling, the dream’s message can be delivered, and its charge can be released. The oil does not take the pain away. It gives you a place to stand while you feel the pain, and that makes all the difference.

After my first business canvas, I felt like I was on Ecstasy.

From Dream to Daylight: Integrating the Night's Wisdom

The work of the dream does not end when you wake up. In many ways, it is just beginning. The insights, the emotions, and the energies that arise in the dreamtime are not just random firings of the brain. They are potent messages from the soul, and they are meant to be brought into the light of day. This is the process of integration, and it is where the real transformation happens. It is not enough to have a powerful dream. You must do something with it. You must allow it to change you.

Sometimes the most honest thing your body can do is be crass, obnoxious, and messy.

Essential oils can be a powerful ally in this process of integration. The same oil that you used as an anchor in the night can be used as a bridge in the day. When you wake, before you even get out of bed, take a moment to breathe in the scent of your chosen oil. As you do, ask yourself: What is the feeling of this dream? What is its central message? What is it asking of me? The scent will help to reactivate the dream state, to bring the memories and emotions of the dream more vividly to the surface. Write down whatever comes. Do not censor it. Do not judge it. Just get it out. This is your raw material.

Throughout the day, you can continue to use the oil as a way to stay connected to the dream’s wisdom. If you find yourself feeling confused or disconnected, take a moment to breathe in the scent. It will act as a reminder, a gentle nudge back to the work that you are doing. You can also use the oil in your meditation or journaling practice. As you sit with the dream, as you write about it, as you explore its meaning, the scent can help to keep you grounded in the felt sense of the experience. It can help you to move beyond a purely intellectual understanding of the dream and into a deeper, more embodied knowing.

The Unfinished Business of How to Use Essential

The work with essential oils and dreams is not a one-time fix. It is a practice, a relationship that you cultivate over time. Some nights, the work will be deep and challenging. Other nights, it will be gentle and illuminating. The point is to keep showing up, to keep engaging with the process with honesty, courage, and a willingness to be changed. You are not just using essential oils to have better dreams. You are using them to become a better dreamer. You are learning to navigate the vast, mysterious, and often challenging landscape of your own inner world. You are learning to be with yourself in a new way, a way that is not about avoiding discomfort, but about finding the truth that is hidden within it.

This is the real work of the soul. It is not always pretty. It is not always comfortable. But it is the only work that matters. It is the work of becoming whole. And it is a work that is available to you every single night, in the sacred and mysterious laboratory of your own dreams. Do not settle for the cheap trick of a pleasant scent. Demand the profound transformation of a courageous engagement. Your dreams are not the enemy. They are the path. And the right use of these potent plant essences can help you walk it with clarity, courage, and a deepening connection to the truth of who you are. So the question is not whether you will use these sacred oils, but if you will use them to truly wake up. Will you continue to perfume the prison of your own denial, or will you use these scents as a key to unlock the door and walk free? The choice, as always, is yours. But know this: your soul is waiting for your answer. Are you ready to stop hiding and start healing?

Go play. Go deep. Be fully and unapologetically YOUR TRUE SELF.

For those interested in the deeper psychological frameworks that underpin this type of work, the International Association for Jungian Studies is an invaluable resource.

Normal is a setting on a washing machine. You, sweetheart, are not a washing machine.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can essential oils cause nightmares?

It is not that essential oils 'cause' nightmares, but they can certainly stir the pot of the subconscious. If you are using an oil with the intention of deep work, it may bring to the surface material that is challenging or unsettling. This is not a bad thing. It is a sign that the work is happening. The key is to be prepared for this, and to have the tools and the mindset to engage with whatever arises, rather than running from it. The nightmare is not the enemy; it is the curriculum.

You don't become enlightened when you fall in love with the color pink — you become enlightened when you rip through your own illusions and kneel humbly before The Divine.

What if I don't remember my dreams?

Dream recall is a muscle. The more you use it, the stronger it gets. The simple act of setting an intention to remember your dreams before you sleep can have a powerful effect. Keep a journal by your bed, and write down anything you can remember upon waking, even if it is just a fragment or a feeling. The ritual of using an essential oil as an anchor can also help to strengthen the bridge between the dreaming and waking worlds, making recall more likely. Be patient, be persistent, and treat the act of remembering as a sacred practice.
